sql.schema_empty: No accessible tables for this user.
sql.safety_note: Defense in depth: PostgreSQL role is read-only with table-level grants · app blocks writes/DDL · single statement · timeout · row cap · parameterized engine (SQL injection mitigated).
sql.security.title: 🔒 Sandboxed database access
sql.security.body: This page connects as {user} — a dedicated read-only PostgreSQL user with SELECT on compliance tables only (no raw landing zone, no writes). Queries pass application-level guards against SQL injection and destructive statements. Questions or access requests: {email}
sql.contact_line: Need help or additional access? Contact: {email}

dq.title: Data Quality
rule_builder.title: Rule Builder
rule_builder.saved: Rules saved. Restart spark-job to apply.
rule_builder.cards_intro: Each card maps to an AML scenario. Toggle enabled rules and adjust thresholds per scenario.
rule_builder.max_txns_hint: Generator injects up to {count} transactions per scenario event.
rule_builder.propose_hint: Describe a scenario you have in mind
rule_builder.propose_placeholder: e.g. Sudden cross-border wires to high-risk corridors after account dormancy...
rule_builder.propose_coming_soon: AI will turn your description into a working scenario and get back to you — coming soon.
scenarios.title: AML Scenarios
scenarios.intro: Eight rule-based money-laundering typologies the streaming engine detects in real time, plus an always-on ML anomaly model (9th card). Each card shows what it catches, the rolling window it evaluates, and the live thresholds — or model stats — currently in force.
scenarios.ml.title: ML Anomaly Detection
scenarios.ml.desc: A self-learning layer that complements the fixed rules: it scores every active customer for unusual behavior instead of checking a single threshold, so it can surface novel patterns the rules were never written for.
scenarios.ml.detect: Isolation Forest flags the top ~{pct}% behavioral outliers over a 30-day window; a supervised model then ranks how alert-worthy each one is. No fixed threshold — the model adapts to the data.
scenarios.ml.details_hint: Full detail on the Risk Models page
scenarios.detect.geographic: Flags transfers involving high-risk or blocked jurisdictions.
scenarios.detect.smurfing: Flags an account with {count}+ transactions under {amount} within a week.
scenarios.detect.daily_velocity: Flags an account exceeding {count} transactions (each ≤ {amount}) in a single day.
scenarios.detect.weekly_volume: Flags an account whose weekly outbound volume exceeds {amount}.
scenarios.detect.monthly_peer: Flags an account exceeding {threshold} txns/month ({baseline} peer baseline × {mult}).
scenarios.detect.dormant: Flags large activity ({amount}+) on a previously dormant account.
scenarios.detect.mule: Flags a receiver collecting from {senders}+ distinct external senders within 24h.
scenarios.detect.generic: Flags transactions matching the configured rule conditions.
health.title: System Health
health.intro: Live operational view of the whole platform — pipeline freshness, Airflow DAGs, source consistency, throughput and storage. All signals read from Postgres.
health.consistency_caption: Live COUNT(*) vs the latest hourly Airflow snapshot. Small drift is expected between snapshots.
pipeline.title: Data Pipeline
pipeline.no_data: No pipeline metrics yet. Run the aml_pipeline_audit Airflow DAG.

retention.title: Data Retention
retention.source_hint: Source of truth: configs/retention.json — edit values there; cleanup_dag enforces hourly.
overview.hero.badge: Real-time Fraud Detection · Data Engineering Showcase
overview.hero.title: Alpha AML — a real-time fraud-detection pipeline, built end to end
overview.hero.sub: A production-style streaming data platform that detects money-laundering and fraud the instant a payment happens. I designed and operate every layer myself — ingestion, streaming analytics, the data model, orchestration and serving — to show how I build reliable, cost-aware big-data systems for the banking & fraud domain. It runs 24/7 on a single 6 GB cloud VM.
overview.numbers.title: At a glance
overview.numbers.live_hint: Live counts from PostgreSQL — same source as Monitoring.
overview.facts.vm: Single cloud-VM footprint — engineered to fit
overview.arch.title: How the pipeline is wired
overview.arch.sub: Eight Docker services, one linear data flow. Each box is a layer I own end to end.
overview.arch.side1: Apache Airflow orchestrates batch jobs alongside the stream: hourly retention cleanup, disk/DB guards and pipeline audits.
overview.arch.side2: GenAI worker drafts the Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) for confirmed cases — the regulatory deliverable.
overview.model.title: The data model — a medallion architecture
overview.model.sub: Raw events are refined layer by layer. The same pattern (Bronze → Silver → Gold) that powers real lakehouse platforms, implemented in PostgreSQL + dbt.
overview.model.bronze.title: Raw, untouched
overview.model.bronze.note: Immutable raw landing zone — the Kafka transactions.raw topic that the generator publishes to and Spark consumes. Bounded by Kafka retention; never persisted as a separate table.
overview.model.silver.title: Conformed & windowed
overview.model.silver.note: Typed, deduplicated transactions plus rolling per-customer window aggregates that the Spark rules read each micro-batch. txn_id is the lineage anchor.
overview.model.gold.title: Business-ready marts
overview.model.gold.note: dbt models for risk scoring and daily fraud reporting — the layer analysts and BI consume. Built with ref()-based lineage and tests.
overview.model.lineage: Lineage is explicit: dbt builds Gold from Silver staging models via ref(), so every reporting figure traces back to a raw event.
overview.eng.title: Engineering decisions that make it work on 6 GB
overview.eng.sub: The interesting part isn't that it runs — it's that it runs reliably inside tight constraints. These are deliberate trade-offs.
overview.eng.e1.title: Cheap counts at scale
overview.eng.e1.body: Dashboard KPIs read PostgreSQL's pg_class.reltuples planner estimates instead of COUNT(*) over 1.2M+ rows — instant cards without hammering the DB.
overview.eng.e2.title: Memory-budgeted containers
overview.eng.e2.body: Every service has a hard mem_limit; Airflow runs a SequentialExecutor on a pre-baked image. Nine services coexist on one VM without OOM.
overview.eng.e3.title: Grant-safe overwrites
overview.eng.e3.body: Spark refreshes aggregate tables with truncate+overwrite (not drop) so read-only SQL grants survive every batch — idempotent, no privilege drift.
overview.eng.e4.title: Alert budgeting
overview.eng.e4.body: Caps (~100/day, max 12 per rule) and one alert per customer/rule/batch keep signal high and protect investigators from alert fatigue.
overview.eng.e5.title: Config-driven retention
overview.eng.e5.body: One JSON file defines every table's lifetime; an Airflow DAG enforces it hourly and an emergency disk guard trims the fastest-growing table if space runs low.
overview.eng.e6.title: Frozen evidence
overview.eng.e6.body: When an alert fires I snapshot the customer's transactions into an archive, so the investigation evidence stays intact even after live history expires.
overview.biz.title: Why a fraud team would care
overview.biz.sub: Built with the domain in mind — the language and outputs a compliance/fraud function actually needs.
overview.biz.b1.title: Audit trail & evidence chain
overview.biz.b1.body: Every alert keeps a frozen, timestamped snapshot of the underlying activity — examiner-ready and reproducible.
overview.biz.b2.title: False-positive control
overview.biz.b2.body: Risk-based priority scoring (rule severity + amount + KYC risk) and alert budgeting focus analyst time on real cases.
overview.biz.b3.title: Regulatory output
overview.biz.b3.body: Auto-drafted SARs mirror the report an FIU expects — the genuine deliverable at the end of an AML investigation.
overview.biz.b4.title: Risk-based monitoring
overview.biz.b4.body: KYC risk scores, PEP flags, customer segments and peer-group baselines drive how transactions are judged — not one-size-fits-all thresholds.
overview.flow.title: The journey of a transaction
overview.flow.sub: Follow one payment from the moment I create it to the moment a compliance report is filed.
overview.flow.s1.title: A payment is born
overview.flow.s1.body: I generate a realistic transaction every 10–20 seconds — sender, receiver, amount, country, currency. This is the endless stream of normal banking activity I simulate.
overview.flow.s2.title: It enters the pipeline
overview.flow.s2.body: Each payment lands on a high-speed event bus that carries huge volumes without losing anything — the backbone of my real-time pipeline.
overview.flow.s3.title: The engine inspects it
overview.flow.s3.body: Every 30 seconds my stream-processing engine reviews recent activity per customer and checks it against money-laundering rules I configured.
overview.flow.s4.title: Suspicious ones are flagged
overview.flow.s4.body: When a payment matches a fraud pattern, I create an alert. Smart caps prevent alert floods so investigators only see what matters.
overview.flow.s5.title: I investigate on the dashboard
overview.flow.s5.body: On this dashboard I review alerts — customer profile, history, risk score — and decide whether a case is truly suspicious.
overview.flow.s6.title: AI drafts the report
overview.flow.s6.body: For confirmed cases, my GenAI worker writes the first draft of the official Suspicious Activity Report (SAR) that banks must send to regulators.
overview.flow.s7.title: Housekeeping runs itself
overview.flow.s7.body: Scheduled jobs I wired with Airflow delete old data on a fixed timetable and guard disk space — no manual babysitting.
overview.scenarios.title: The 8 AML typologies I detect
overview.scenarios.sub: Each maps to a recognised money-laundering typology. A scenario scheduler injects them at random so every pattern fires at least once per week — giving the rule engine realistic positives to catch.
overview.sc.geo.title: Geographic / sanctions risk
overview.sc.geo.body: Funds to or from FATF high-risk or sanctioned jurisdictions (e.g. Russia, North Korea).
overview.sc.hv.title: High-value anomaly
overview.sc.hv.body: A single transfer far above the customer's normal range — a classic threshold red flag.
overview.sc.smurf.title: Structuring (smurfing)
overview.sc.smurf.body: Splitting a large sum into many sub-threshold payments to evade reporting limits.
overview.sc.velocity.title: Transaction velocity spike
overview.sc.velocity.body: A sudden burst of many outgoing payments within a short window.
overview.sc.weekly.title: Weekly volume escalation
overview.sc.weekly.body: Cumulative weekly transfer volume climbing past the customer's expected limit.
overview.sc.peer.title: Peer-group anomaly
overview.sc.peer.body: Behaviour far outside the baseline of statistically similar customers in the same segment.
overview.sc.dormant.title: Dormant reactivation
overview.sc.dormant.body: A long-inactive account suddenly moving large amounts — a sleeper-account signal.
overview.sc.mule.title: Money-mule fan-in
overview.sc.mule.body: Many unrelated senders funnelling money into one collection (mule) account.
overview.tech.title: The stack, by layer
overview.tech.sub: Industry-standard tools, each mapped to the layer it owns — the way a data platform is actually organised.
overview.tech.python: generates data and wires the rules
overview.data.title: Customers, data & how I keep it healthy
overview.data.cust.title: Customers & addresses
overview.data.cust.body: I seeded ~1.23M simulated customers, each with 1–5 addresses, KYC risk scores and segments. 5–8 new customers join every day. This master data is permanent.
overview.data.budget.title: Alert budgeting
overview.data.budget.body: I cap alerts (≈100/day, max 12 per rule) and emit one alert per customer per rule per batch — preventing noise and alert fatigue.
overview.data.retention.title: Data retention
overview.data.retention.body: Old data expires automatically on a schedule I defined in one config file, so the database never grows out of control.
overview.data.guard_caption: I also guard disk & memory: an Airflow job checks every 15 minutes and emergency-trims the fastest-growing table if space runs low.
overview.footer: Alpha AML — a real-time fraud-detection data pipeline I built to showcase big-data, streaming, SQL and orchestration engineering. Explore the live system via the sidebar: Monitoring, Investigation, SAR Archive, Rule Builder and a read-only SQL Explorer.
footer.raw: Txn 90d | Alerts/SAR 180d | Raw 24h | Metrics 4h | DAG cleanup_dag — next UTC:
rm.title: Risk Models
rm.intro: ML risk layer: an unsupervised Isolation Forest scores every active customer for behavioral anomaly, and a supervised triage model learns the rule-engine alert label. Scored nightly by the ml_score Airflow DAG.
rm.help_title: New here? How to read this page
rm.help_body:
**The idea.** The rule engine is a checklist: it fires when a transaction crosses a fixed threshold (e.g. "more than €10k in a day"). That is precise but blind to anything no one wrote a rule for. This page adds a **machine-learning layer** that learns what *normal* looks like from the data itself, so it can flag customers whose overall behavior is simply unusual.

**Two models work together:**

- **Isolation Forest (unsupervised).** It gets no labels — it just learns the shape of typical 30-day behavior (transaction count, volume, amount spread, counterparties, cross-border share, etc.). Each customer gets an **anomaly score** from 0 to 1; the highest ~5% are tagged anomalies. Think of it as "how far from the crowd is this customer?"
- **Gradient Boosting (supervised triage).** This one *does* see labels — it learns which customers the rule engine alerted on, then predicts how alert-worthy each customer is. It helps **rank** the anomalies so an analyst looks at the most promising ones first.

**How to judge it.** ROC-AUC and PR-AUC (below) measure how well the triage model separates alert-worthy customers from the rest — 0.5 is a coin flip, 1.0 is perfect. The overlap panel shows where the model and the rules agree or disagree. "ML only" cases are the interesting ones: behavior the fixed thresholds missed.

**Important:** this layer is for **insight and prioritisation only** — it does *not* create real alerts in the system. It is the always-on 9th scenario on the Scenarios page.
rm.cap_dist: How to read it: most customers sit on the left (normal). A long right tail, especially the rule-flagged colour, means the model and the rules see eye to eye on the riskiest accounts.
rm.cap_roc: Closer to the top-left corner is better. The diagonal line is random guessing — the further the curve bows above it, the better the model tells alert-worthy customers apart.
rm.cap_pr: Useful when alerts are rare: high precision at high recall means the model finds real cases without drowning analysts in false positives.
rm.cap_importance: Which behavioral signals drive the triage score most. Longer bars = the model leans on that feature more when deciding who looks alert-worthy.
rm.cap_overlap: Bottom line: 'Both' = model confirms the rules; 'ML only' = new leads the thresholds missed; 'Rule only' = policy hits that aren't statistically unusual.
rm.no_run: No model run yet. Trigger the ml_score Airflow DAG to train and score.
rm.dist_caption: Isolation Forest anomaly score (0–1, higher = more anomalous), split by whether the rule engine also flagged the customer.
rm.overlap_caption: Customer-level agreement between the static rule engine and the ML anomaly model. 'ML only' are behavioral outliers the thresholds missed; 'Rule only' are deterministic policy hits the model did not flag as anomalous.
log.title: System Event Logs
log.intro: Centralized WARNING+ feed from every service (generator, Spark, SAR, ML) plus Airflow DAG failures — one place to see what is going wrong.
log.none: No events match the current filters.
analytics.title: Analytics
analytics.intro: Trend and breakdown analysis across transactions, alerts, customer acquisition, segments, cross-border corridors and risk — built from the same operational data the engine runs on.
analytics.no_data: No data available for this section yet.
analytics.s1_insight: Daily transaction count (bars) against EUR volume (line) — watch for volume spikes that are not matched by count, i.e. larger-than-usual transfers.
analytics.s2_insight: Daily alerts (bars) and alerts per 1,000 transactions (line). A rising ratio means the engine is flagging a larger share of activity — tune thresholds if it drifts.
analytics.s3_insight: New customers per day, stacked by onboarding channel — shows growth pace and the channel mix feeding KYC risk.
analytics.s4_insight: 30-day volume and alert rate broken down by segment, onboarding channel and transaction type — high alert-rate / low-volume segments deserve attention.
analytics.s5_insight: Top sender → receiver country corridors by 30-day volume, shaded by alert count — concentration in high-risk corridors is a classic laundering signal.
analytics.s6_insight: Incidence is measured among active customers (those who transacted in the last 30 days) — it should rise with the KYC risk band and PEP status; if it does not, the risk model and rules may be mis-calibrated.
